replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: Thank you for coming back and sharing such an incredibly honest, firsthand perspective from your time living and traveling across Pakistan in the 80s and 90s. 🙏📜 Witnessing iconic cities like Peshawar, Lahore, and Karachi during those specific decades gives you a deep, realistic view of the region's complex history. You are completely right—reality on the ground often carries heavy layers that a documentary can only skim. A lot has indeed changed over the decades, both socially and structurally. We deeply appreciate you bringing your real-life memories to our thread! What do you feel is the biggest change you've noticed or heard about since your last time there in 1990?

comments.author: @GlobalRealLifeDocumentary
comments.text: Pakistan completely shattered so many stereotypes for me—towering mountains, ancient civilizations, incredible hospitality, and breathtaking landscapes. 🇵🇰 After watching this documentary, what changed your perspective the most, and which place in Pakistan would you love to visit first?

replies.author: @exo-gamerz2662
replies.text: Pakistan has been demonised out of proportion by our neighbours. Admittedly, our governments and politicians contributed to tarnished images. Our greed and lack of empathy and callousness have left disastrous impacts on our global images. Our greed and dishonesty have turned our country into a beggary. It is biased to say it is a threat to any country. Nonsense. A struggling country can't be a threat though a nuclear power. Nevertheless, it has the capacity to repulse a bigger power than itself. This is a plus point. 😊

replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: You've raised an incredibly sharp and spot-on point! 🌍✨ Media bias often creates a massive gap between a country's global 'image' and the actual reality on the ground. Anyone who has truly traveled through Pakistan knows that the overwhelming warmth, hospitality, and peace found among ordinary citizens completely defy the old headlines. It's an image problem, not a safety one. We completely agree! What do you think is the best way for creators to help bridge this gap and show the true side of the country?

replies.author: @reallifeincountries

Hearing the stories of your generation and your mother’s education brings a crucial piece of authentic local context that mainstream media often overlooks. You raise a very fair and powerful point regarding the deep respect for women’s dignity within Pakistani culture. We deeply appreciate you helping us and our community see these nuanced cultural perspectives. What do you feel is the biggest thing people outside Pakistan misunderstand about the daily resilience of women in Lyari?"
